pattern: Whether a target day lets you build enough bouquets is a yes/no question that only gets easier as the day gets later, which is exactly the shape binary search is built for.
hints: For a fixed candidate day, you can check in one linear scan whether enough bouquets can be formed: walk the garden counting consecutive already-bloomed flowers and cash in a bouquet every time you reach k in a row.
That feasibility check only gets easier (never harder) as the candidate day increases, so binary search over the possible days instead of testing every one.

Search between the earliest and latest bloom day for the smallest day whose feasibility check succeeds; first confirm m*k does not exceed the number of flowers, or it's impossible no matter how long you wait.

edge_inputs.description: single-flower bouquets, wait for the third flower to bloom
not enough flowers exist for the requested bouquets even if every one blooms
adjacency requirement rules out an early day with enough total blooms but no adjacent run
every flower already blooms on day 1
exactly enough flowers for one bouquet using the whole garden
a single very late flower splits an otherwise-ready garden into two separate runs
one bouquet worth of flowers exactly, so the day must equal the slowest flower in that run

stress.description: near-max garden size with a feasible target
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    bloomDay = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, min(50, n))
    m = max(1, n // (2 * k))
    return [bloomDay, m, k]

stress.description: near-max garden size where the request is impossible
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, size)
    bloomDay = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, min(50, n))
    m = n
    return [bloomDay, m, k]
